claim: Strict competition-policy enforcement (EU DG-Comp, Bundeskartellamt) produces higher consumer welfare than laissez-faire antitrust (US post-Bork consumer-welfare-standard narrowing) in sectors prone to concentration.

covers_claims_inbound.claim_text: Strict competition-policy enforcement (EU DG-Comp, Bundeskartellamt) produces higher consumer welfare than laissez-faire antitrust (US post-Bork consumer-welfare-standard narrowing) in sectors prone to concentration.

Reagan-era and Trump-era US antitrust enforcement retreat produced measurable concentration increases and markup rises in US product markets over 1980–2020, consistent with the Ordoliberal prediction that markets absent competition enforcement trend toward concentration.
covers_claims_inbound.claim_notes: Audit flagged as FAIL vs current hypothesis (which is EU-strict-vs-US-lax cross-country, whereas this claim is US-only time-series of retreat episodes). Phase 4C candidate for new hypothesis: us_antitrust_retreat_concentration_effect.

claim: Deregulation episodes (US transportation 1978–1980, UK telecoms 1984, NZ Rogernomics 1984–1993) show measurable TFP gains in the deregulated sectors within a decade.

covers_claims_inbound.claim_text: Deregulation episodes (US transportation 1978–1980, UK telecoms 1984, NZ Rogernomics 1984–1993) show measurable TFP gains in the deregulated sectors within a decade.

claim: The cumulative EU regulatory stack (GDPR 2018, MiFID II 2018, DMA 2022, DSA 2022, AI Act 2024, Taxonomy Regulation 2020, CSRD 2023, MiCA 2023, CBAM 2023, plus sector-specific accretion) has imposed measurable productivity drag on EU firms relative to US counterparts in the same sectors. The drag is concentrated in scale-sensitive sectors (digital platforms, asset management, large manufacturing, financial services) where fixed compliance costs are spread over smaller market size than US peers achieve. Over 2018-2023, the cumulative TFP differential between EU and US in affected sectors exceeds 5 percentage points. This is consistent with the Draghi Report (2024) characterisation of EU competitiveness decline.

covers_claims_inbound.claim_text: EU regulatory burden (REACH, GDPR, AI Act, CSRD) produces measurable productivity drag relative to lighter-regulation peer economies over 2000–2020, consistent with classical-liberal theory on compliance deadweight.
covers_claims_inbound.claim_notes: Audit flagged as FAIL — claim scope references REACH (pre-2015) alongside GDPR/AI Act/CSRD and uses 2000–2020 window; linked hypothesis named entities are GDPR, MiFID II, DMA, DSA, AI Act, Taxonomy, CSRD, MiCA, CBAM within 2015–2023 window (REACH absent). Phase 4C: candidate for claim rewrite to drop REACH and align 2015–2023 window, or hypothesis augmentation to cover pre-2015 regulatory stack including REACH.

claim: Major episodes of financial deregulation — the 1999 US Gramm-Leach- Bliley repeal of Glass-Steagall, the 1986 UK Financial Services Act ("Big Bang"), Chile's 1974-1981 banking liberalisation, Sweden's late-1980s credit-market liberalisation, and Japan's 1996-2001 Big Bang — are followed within 10 years by higher-than-baseline incidence of banking crises, measured by the Laeven-Valencia Systemic Banking Crisis Database, and by elevated credit-to-GDP gaps per BIS. The hypothesis is that deregulation expands credit frontiers faster than prudential oversight adapts, producing a transitional vulnerability window; it does not claim deregulation causes a crisis in every case, only that the hazard rate is materially elevated relative to matched non-deregulating country-years.

covers_claims_inbound.claim_text: Post-1980 financial deregulation in the US and UK (Depository Institutions Act 1980, UK Big Bang 1986, Gramm-Leach-Bliley 1999) increased the frequency and severity of financial crises through 2008.

The 2007-2009 global financial crisis demonstrates systemic instability of capitalist accumulation, with deeper output contraction in liberalised financial systems (US, UK) than in more state-managed ones (China, Vietnam).
covers_claims_inbound.claim_notes: Audit flagged as FAIL vs current hypothesis (which tests OECD deregulation episodes — Glass-Steagall 1999, UK Big Bang 1986, Chile 1974-81, Sweden late-80s, Japan Big Bang — producing elevated banking-crisis hazard; whereas this claim tests GFC contraction-depth in liberalised US/UK vs state-managed China/Vietnam). Phase 4C candidate for new hypothesis: gfc_contraction_depth_liberalised_vs_state_managed_systems.

covers_claims_inbound.claim_text: The 2007-2009 financial crisis originated in endogenous leverage build-up (Minsky hedge-speculative-Ponzi transition) within financial sector balance sheets, not in exogenous shocks.
covers_claims_inbound.claim_notes: Phase 4C candidate: linked hypothesis (financial_deregulation_crisis_vulnerability) tests deregulation-as-cause mechanism across 1970-2023, while this claim is a narrower endogenous-Minsky-mechanism proposition for GFC specifically. Claim and hypothesis are adjacent but distinct causal chains. Consider splitting into new hypothesis: gfc_endogenous_minsky_leverage_mechanism.

covers_claims_inbound.claim_text: Financial deregulation across OECD 1980-2008 (Big Bang 1986, Gramm-Leach-Bliley 1999) raised crisis frequency above the stable 1945-1980 regulated-finance era.
covers_claims_inbound.claim_notes: Phase 4C candidate: audit flagged POLICY_NAMED_ABSENT (claim cites Gramm-Leach-Bliley while hypothesis entities list Glass-Steagall); LLM verdict confirms substantive match since Gramm-Leach-Bliley is the repeal of Glass-Steagall. Hypothesis entity extraction should be updated to include gramm_leach_bliley_1999 alongside glass_steagall.

claim: New Zealand post-1984 Rogernomics reforms delivered growth improvement only in conjunction with later institutional reforms (Reserve Bank Act 1989, Fiscal Responsibility Act 1994), not from deregulation alone.

covers_claims_inbound.claim_text: New Zealand post-1984 Rogernomics reforms delivered growth improvement only in conjunction with later institutional reforms (Reserve Bank Act 1989, Fiscal Responsibility Act 1994), not from deregulation alone.

claim: Statutory price ceilings set below market-clearing prices reliably produce shortages, rationing via queue or privilege, quality degradation, and black-market arbitrage — across every documented episode where enforcement is sustained. The empirical record is uniform: US wage-price controls 1971-1974 (Nixon freeze + Phase II-IV), US oil price controls 1973-1981 (gasoline lines + shortage crises), Venezuela 2003-present (chronic shortage + CPI-deflated official prices diverging from parallel-market prices by orders of magnitude), Zimbabwe 2007-2008 (shop-shelf emptying), Argentina Precios Cuidados 2014-2023 (product disappearance), Soviet peacetime price controls (queueing equilibrium). No documented sustained binding price ceiling has produced improved allocation outcomes at policy-relevant scale.

covers_claims_inbound.claim_text: Price controls produce shortages and quality degradation.
covers_claims_inbound.claim_notes: ML position predicts planning can allocate without price signals; framework predicts shortages.
